title: Plain Paratha

overview.description: A layered Indian flatbread made from wholemeal atta, folded with ghee or oil and cooked on a hot tawa until crisp outside and soft inside.

ingredients: 1 tbsp neutral oil or melted ghee
Extra oil or ghee, for layering and cooking
method: Mix the atta and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the warm water, mixing until a soft dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until smooth and pliable.
Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes.
Divide the dough into 6 equal portions and roll each into a smooth ball.
Roll one ball into a thin circle.
Brush the surface lightly with oil or ghee and dust with a little atta.
Fold the dough into a square or roll it into a loose spiral to create layers.
Flatten gently and roll again into a thick, roughly circular disc.
Heat a dry tawa or heavy-based frying pan over medium heat.
Place the paratha on the hot pan.
Cook until the surface begins to develop bubbles and the underside has pale brown spots.
Turn and brush the cooked surface lightly with oil or ghee.
Turn again and brush the other side.
Cook until both sides are golden brown and crisp in places.
Gently press and scrunch the paratha between your hands or tap the edges to separate the layers.
Keep warm in a clean tea towel.

learning.did-you-know: The word paratha is often linked to the idea of layered or folded bread, reflecting the technique used to create its characteristic flaky texture.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How fat and folding create layers in a flatbread and how to cook a richer dough so it becomes crisp without drying out.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A useful progression from chapati and roti that introduces layering and the effect of fat on the texture of flatbread.
chapman-tips: Resting the dough makes it much easier to roll and helps prevent it springing back.
Use enough fat to create layers but avoid making the dough greasy.
Don't worry about making perfect circles; the folding and rolling process naturally creates irregular shapes.
Roll the finished paratha thicker than a chapati or roti.
Cook over medium heat so the layers have time to cook through before the outside burns.
Gently scrunching the cooked paratha helps separate the layers and makes it more flaky.
notes.batch-cooking: Cooked paratha freeze well. Cool completely, stack with baking paper between them if desired and freeze in an airtight bag or container. Reheat directly from frozen in a dry frying pan.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover paratha as a wrap, cut into pieces for dipping or reheat and serve with eggs.
